## [00:00 - 03:03]

What are the most common attribution mistakes that advertisers make and what can they do to fix them? So this is a continuation of a series that I'm doing on common mistakes. And you can read the blog post I wrote on attribution at johnloomer.com slash attribution. Before we get to those five most common mistakes, little ground rules, we got to define what attribution is. So this is essentially how meta gives credit to an ad for conversion. It usually happens on your website, but it doesn't have to when it comes to attribution, it spills down from the top. If you get it wrong, it impacts your reporting, the optimization, and you're targeting. It can screw everything up. And when it comes to those five most common mistakes, they usually fall into the categories of setup. So you set up everything wrong that leads to proper attribution application. So you don't know how to apply attribution in your advertising, interpreting your results. So what does it actually mean is this result is valuable? Is this other result? And just a general understanding of how everything works. So critical. And it leads to lots of problems. So let's go through those five. Some of these might be obvious. I think some of them may not be. Number one improper or incomplete setup of the pixel or conversions API, maybe both. So we're not even getting into the events at this point. But it's the pixel and the conversions API that pass information to meta, so that ads manager will know whether someone who saw or clicked on your ad actually did something that is really important. So did you get this setup properly? Did you set it up manually? Did you use a third party tool? What did you do? Now there was a time when just using the meta pixel was enough when it was called the Facebook pixel. But then everything that happened with iOS 14 and tracking blocking and privacy and everything else made the conversions API that much more important to pass first party data. So when it comes to conversions API, you have the web API and if all of your business is done online, that might be all that you need. And the offline or CRM API. So maybe someone becomes a lead, you have a salesperson who calls them, they close the deal, the purchase happens and it didn't happen online. You need to pass that to meta too, so that you can have that reflected potentially in your ads. And there are multiple ways to get this setup. There's not just one way. But the main thing is this provides a much fuller picture of the conversions that's happening. Number two, not surprising, failure of standard events,

## [03:03 - 06:04]

custom events, custom conversions. So first of all, it's just a matter of misunderstanding their roles and unique purpose. I think it's a very high percentage of advertisers who do not understand the difference between these three things who think that one can be used instead of another in all cases. But the reality is that they have three very unique roles. Stair and events are predefined conversion types that met already has. So purchase, complete registration, lead, add to cart, on and on and on. If you can use a standard event, use a standard event. It defines any purchase on your website, not for a specific product necessarily, but any purchase would be an example. Now you may have other important actions that happen on your website. That is where custom events come in, because there is no predefined bucket that makes sense. I use custom events because I think other actions are really important. Like how long you spend on the website, whether you scroll down the page, whether you watch an embedded YouTube video, there is a long list of custom events that I use that I track as well. And then custom conversions. Custom conversions and custom events are not the same thing. Oh my goodness, advertisers use these two terms interchangeably. Custom conversions merely provide detail regarding the events that happened. If you're running a campaign in ads manager, you have a results column for purchases. That's not necessarily going to be purchases of the thing that you're selling. The vast majority of those purchases probably are. But if you had a custom conversion, you can add a column that indicates it's a purchase of this specific product. So you can use custom conversions, both to map towards standard events and the details of that standard event and custom events as well. The failure of understanding improper incomplete setup, not setting up events for all of your important actions. And then there's over and under reporting because you set it up improperly. Maybe you have the event firing on the wrong page or during the wrong step. How many times have we seen that? It's like, Oh, Meta is saying we've got all these purchases happen and never happened. Maybe you have this setup wrong. Usually that's the case. It's not that Meta is inflating your numbers. It has something to do with the way that you set it up. So that's number two, number three, inability to understand the meaning of your conversion results. So typical advertiser will look at their results. They just look at the results column, and they take it at face value. But the reality is there are several attribution settings that contribute to those results. There's seven day click, one day view by default. So anyone who clicks your ad and converts within seven days, anyone who views your ad without clicking within

## [06:04 - 09:07]

one day are counted within those results for any of your standard events, custom events, custom conversions, any of your conversion events. ## [09:07 - 09:52]

impact your default reporting, it will impact the way your ads are delivered, because meta wants to get you results. Those results aren't going to happen if you turn off one day view. So you still may get one day view, but not in your default reporting, you'll need to go back and compare attribution settings again to see those other conversions that happen. So maybe down the road you say, Oh, a bunch of them are still coming in at seven day click, 28 day click, but it's not in your default reporting.
